edges.description: Autoimmune demyelination and neuronal loss lead to progressive neurological disability.
Autoreactive Th17 cells migrate through the BBB and drive focal demyelinating lesion formation.

nodes.description: Used to reduce inflammation and speed recovery during acute exacerbations.

evidence.reference_title: Corticosteroids for multiple sclerosis: I. Application for treating exacerbations.
evidence.snippet: The treatment of MS exacerbations with anti-inflammatory agents such as corticosteroids and adrenocorticotropic hormone has represented an established practice throughout the neurology community.

evidence.reference_title: Immunologic therapy of multiple sclerosis.
evidence.snippet: Three interferon beta preparations (Betaseron, Avonex, and Rebif) have shown efficacy in the treatment of relapsing-remitting multiple sclerosis (MS).

evidence.reference_title: Evoked potentials in multiple sclerosis before and after high-dose methylprednisolone infusion.
evidence.snippet: There were no manifest changes of the evoked potentials parameters parallel to the clinical effect of high-dose therapy.

nodes.description: The immune system attacks and destroys myelin, the protective sheath around nerve fibers, disrupting nerve signal transmission.

biological_processes: Antigen processing and presentation of peptide antigen via MHC class II

evidence.reference_title: Immune regulation of multiple sclerosis.
evidence.snippet: Multiple sclerosis (MS) is considered a prototype inflammatory autoimmune disorder of the central nervous system (CNS)... These autoreactive lymphocytes can migrate to the CNS where they become reactivated upon encountering their target antigen, initiating an autoimmune inflammatory attack. This ultimately leads to demyelination and axonal damage.

evidence.reference_title: Oligodendrocytes: biology and pathology.
evidence.snippet: Oligodendrocytes are the myelinating cells of the central nervous system (CNS)... we will lay out the different pathways leading to oligodendrocyte and myelin loss in human CNS diseases...

evidence.reference_title: Schwann Cell Remyelination in the Multiple Sclerosis Central Nervous System.
evidence.snippet: Multiple sclerosis (MS) is a central nervous system (CNS) demyelinating disease... CNS myelin is normally produced by oligodendroglial cells.

evidence.reference_title: Alterations of Oligodendrocyte and Myelin Energy Metabolism in Multiple Sclerosis.
evidence.snippet: Multiple sclerosis (MS) is a complex autoimmune disease of the central nervous system (CNS), characterized by demyelination and neurodegeneration. Oligodendrocytes play a vital role in maintaining the integrity of myelin... However, in MS, oligodendrocytes become dysfunctional, leading to myelin damage and axonal degeneration.

evidence.reference_title: Lessons from the neuropathology of atypical forms of multiple sclerosis.
evidence.snippet: Multiple sclerosis (MS) is characterized by multiple demyelinated inflammatory lesions disseminated in the central nervous system (CNS)... different mechanisms of demyelination, such as T-cell/macrophage-mediated demyelination, antibody/complement-mediated demyelination, and primary damage of the oligodendrocyte have been observed in individual MS patients.

evidence.reference_title: Targeting oligodendrocyte protection and remyelination in multiple sclerosis.
evidence.snippet: Multiple sclerosis is an inflammatory demyelinating disease of the brain and spinal cord with a presumed autoimmune etiology... Approaches that directly protect myelin-producing oligodendrocytes and enhance remyelination may improve long-term outcomes...

evidence.reference_title: Multiple Sclerosis: Basic and Clinical.
evidence.snippet: MS is a chronic inflammatory disease of the central nervous system caused by aberrant immune activation resulting in damage to myelin sheaths within the brain and spinal cord and axonal loss

evidence.reference_title: Pathogenesis of myelin/oligodendrocyte damage in multiple sclerosis.
evidence.snippet: Substantial evidence supports autoimmune activity as the etiologic mechanism underlying multiple sclerosis (MS)... Both the innate and the adaptive arms of the immune system are involved in the aberrant response to several antigens associated with the myelin sheath and oligodendrocytes (OGCs)...

evidence.reference_title: Demyelination in multiple sclerosis.
evidence.snippet: This review, focused on demyelination in multiple sclerosis, is divided in two parts. The first part addresses the many and not exclusive mechanisms leading to demyelination in the central nervous system... the influence of a primary immune response against myelin antigen(s), with a diversity of potential targets.

nodes.description: Medications that can slow the progression of the disease and reduce the frequency and severity of relapses.

evidence.reference_title: Current disease-modifying therapies in multiple sclerosis.
evidence.snippet: In recent years, the usefulness of interferon beta and glatiramer acetate in the treatment of relapsing-remitting multiple sclerosis (RRMS) has been established.

evidence.reference_title: Multiple sclerosis update.
evidence.snippet: Diseasemodifying drugs (DMDs) that reduce the frequency of relapses, development of brain lesions, and progression of disability are the standard of care for relapsing forms of MS, and the use of DMDs should be initiated as early as possible

evidence.reference_title: Effects of multiple sclerosis disease-modifying therapies on employment measures using patient-reported data.
evidence.snippet: Those using the higher efficacy (category 3) DMTs, particularly fingolimod and natalizumab, reported significant increases in amount of work, work attendance and work productivity, suggesting they have important beneficial effects on work life in people with MS.

evidence.reference_title: Disease modifying therapies for relapsing multiple sclerosis.
evidence.snippet: Disease modifying therapies (DMTs) approved for relapsing multiple sclerosis interfere with a variety of immunological mechanisms to reduce rates of relapse, accumulation of disease burden measured by magnetic resonance imaging (MRI), and decline in neurological function over the two to three year duration of typical randomized controlled trials.

evidence.reference_title: Approved and Emerging Disease Modifying Therapies on Neurodegeneration in Multiple Sclerosis.
evidence.snippet: Several FDA-approved medications seek to alleviate disease progression by reducing the impact of such factors as demyelination and neurodegeneration.

nodes.description: Immune cells crossing the blood-brain barrier create focal areas of inflammation in the central nervous system.

evidence.reference_title: How do immune cells overcome the blood-brain barrier in multiple sclerosis?
evidence.snippet: Leukocyte entry into the CNS is nonetheless an early event in multiple sclerosis (MS), an inflammatory disorder of the CNS... Immune cells of MS subjects express inflammatory cytokines, reactive oxygen species (ROS) and enzymes that can facilitate their migration to the CNS by influencing BBB function, either directly or indirectly.

evidence.reference_title: What Guides Peripheral Immune Cells into the Central Nervous System?
evidence.snippet: During this early stage of the disease, leukocytes cross the blood-brain barrier to drive the formation of focal demyelinating plaques.

evidence.reference_title: [Actualidades en la inmunopatología de la esclerosis múltiple].
evidence.snippet: Neuroinflammation is triggered when peripheral leukocytes migrate to the central nervous system and release cytokines such as interleukins 1 and 6 (IL-1 and 6) and tumor necrosis factor (TNF), which act on dwelling cells.

evidence.reference_title: The Role of Astrocytes in Multiple Sclerosis.
evidence.snippet: Astrocytes are now recognized to be early and highly active players during lesion formation and key for providing peripheral immune cells access to the central nervous system.

nodes.description: MS immunopathology is mediated by myelin-reactive CD4+ T cells of Th1 and Th17 lineage. Th17 cells produce IL-17, IL-22, and other proinflammatory cytokines that disrupt blood-brain barrier tight junction proteins and recruit neutrophils into the CNS. The IL-23/IL-17 axis amplifies neuroinflammation.

evidence.reference_title: The role of TH17 cells in multiple sclerosis: Therapeutic implications.
evidence.snippet: Multiple sclerosis (MS) is an inflammatory demyelinating disease of the central nervous system (CNS) where immunopathology is thought to be mediated by myelin-reactive CD4+ T helper (TH) cells. The TH cells most commonly implicated in the pathogenesis of the disease are of TH1 and TH17 lineage, which are defined by the production of interferon-γ and interleukin-17, respectively.

evidence.reference_title: Th17 cell, the new player of neuroinflammatory process in multiple sclerosis.
evidence.snippet: Multiple sclerosis (MS) is an autoimmune disease characterized by recurrent episodes of demyelination and axonal lesion mediated by CD4(+) T cells with a proinflammatory Th1 and Th17 phenotype, macrophages, and soluble inflammatory mediators.

evidence.reference_title: Th17 cell, the new player of neuroinflammatory process in multiple sclerosis.
evidence.snippet: Autoreactive Th17 cells can migrate through the BBB by the production of cytokines such as IL-17 and IL-22, which disrupt tight junction proteins in the central nervous system (CNS) endothelial cells.
